title: Phone number entry vs text input vs input mask vs inline validation vs error summary
summary: Use phone number entry when the product needs a callable or textable contact route with telephone keyboard, purpose text, contact-choice recovery, familiar-format tolerance, review, and optional access confirmation; use text input for ordinary short text, input mask only for narrow fixed formats, inline validation for local correction, and error summary for failed submitted forms.
decisionRules: Prefer phone number entry when the value will be used to call, text, identify, recover, confirm, or route a user through a telephone network.
Prefer text input when the value is a name, label, username, reference, or contact preference that must not be parsed as a telephone number.

Prefer input mask only when the service accepts a narrow domestic phone-number format with no international numbers, extensions, short codes, or assistive exceptions; otherwise use tolerant phone number entry with validation after entry.

Prefer inline validation inside phone number entry for missing number, too few digits, unsupported country code, non-SMS-capable number in an SMS flow, extension handling, or ambiguous contact method.
Prefer error summary when a submitted appointment, account, or contact form has several errors and users need a top-of-form list that links back to the phone field.

Use type tel and autocomplete tel for a complete phone number; use tel-country-code, tel-national, tel-area-code, tel-local, or tel-extension only when the product deliberately splits the number into parts.
Do not use type number because phone numbers are identifiers, not arithmetic values; leading zeros, plus signs, spaces, parentheses, and extensions can be meaningful to users.
Tell users whether you need UK, international, mobile, landline, SMS-capable, or extension-bearing numbers, and explain when the organization may call or text.
Allow users to enter familiar formatting and paste from contacts; trim harmless whitespace but avoid silently rewriting the display value or forcing an unfamiliar international format into the field.
Offer another contact route when users do not have a phone, cannot use one, share one, cannot receive SMS, or need relay, email, post, or support contact.
Review the exact phone number and contact method before consequential use, and separately store the dialable canonical value used by telephony systems.
Use phone confirmation only when phone access must be proven; do not force SMS verification for low-risk contact fields or for landline-only users.
failureModes: A form stores the phone as a number and strips a leading zero or plus sign.
A domestic mask rejects international numbers, extensions, or pasted numbers with spaces and parentheses.
A service asks for a phone number without explaining why it is needed or when the user might be contacted.
A sign-in flow forces SMS verification and blocks users with landlines, shared phones, inaccessible devices, or relay needs.
A reformatter changes the displayed number so users no longer recognize what they entered.
A top-of-page error says invalid value without linking back to the phone field or preserving the typed number.

sources.note: GOV.UK supports only collecting needed phone numbers, contact choice, clear number type and purpose, type tel, autocomplete tel, specific errors, avoiding input masks, and avoiding confusing reformatting.
ONS supports clear phone-number type, purpose and contact timing, familiar formats, and missing or incorrect error details above the field.
MDN supports telephone input semantics, no built-in global format validation, mobile telephone keyboards, and fallback to text input.
MDN supports tel autocomplete and component tokens for split telephone number fields.
ITU E.164 supports canonical international phone-number storage with a 15-digit maximum international number length.
